segments.text: Reitata.
… Starts really, it's going to be touching.
Class is going to be a touch here.
O, I'm sorry.
O, good afternoon minister and the posters will come to the social services in the community
so, it's very short.
We have you for 15 minutes this afternoon, so I look forward to making a brief, I think
statement.
Please, all in favour, the minister will make more Questions.
Sure, thank you Mr Chair.
Obviously Kelly Coombs has no introduction.
is my maralisa CEO of the Ministry for Women. Over the past year, we think that the government has
actually made some really good progress towards improving outcomes for women and goals, particularly
across employment, education, health, wellbeing, leadership and safety. My number one priority
as the Minister is responsible is to improve women's economic empowerment. There is obviously
a number of work streams that I have got underway which I will go through shortly.
But from a whole of government perspective, I'm really pleased to say that we have since forming a
government legislatorate for tax relief for the first time in 14 years we've increased
paid parental leave by 10.74% and we've introduced 25% rebate on childcare costs for some families
we've raised their abatement threshold and rate for families that qualify for working
for family support. Key to improving women's economic empowerment is driving down that pay gap
and driving women into highly paid leadership roles. So, I was really pleased to note that the
latest stock take for public sector boards and committee appointments shows that women's
representation across public sector governance remains really strong for the fifth consecutive
year sits over 50%. They hold just over 52% of public sector board roles and at chair positions,
44.5%. At my instruction, the ministry is undertaking a refresh of the north
nominations service that it runs, and that shares public sector board vacancies with its members
and nominates suitably qualified women to appointing agencies. It's actually now working on a way
to launch that service out to the private sector as well, and I've also decided to set a target
to increase the number of women in the database each year by about 20%. That means that there
will be more emerging talent being appointed across both public and private sectors.
Of course, gender pay gaps do continue to impact women, and following the launch of the first
ever government-backed gender pay gap toolkit in November last year, I'm pleased to say we've had
about 24,000 visits to our website. I've been leading a series of roadshows around the nation,
so far we've done six. We have plenty more planned and underway. Online harm is very clearly
a problem that is preventing women from stepping into public facing leadership roles. Last month,
I launched an online harm prevention toolkit to support women to engage online with confidence,
and it's being developed by the Ministry for Women alongside NetSafe, and it includes
modules with practical guidance to help women navigate incidents of online harm alongside
their employers. The intent of this particular toolkit is to ensure that this kind of harm
doesn't create barriers to women stepping into those leadership and public facing roles.
Over the past year, the Ministry has continued to support claimant participation in the Manawahine
Kopapa inquiry alongside Tupuna Kokiri. The hearings are now underway, and will continue
through until next year. We will continue to focus on our work across government to maintain
women's equal representation on public sector boards, lifting women's incomes, and addressing
the gender pay gap. Other areas of focus will include supporting women and girls to have good
health and wellbeing by increasing awareness of women's health issues through tools,
information and resources. Thank you.
Mr Chair, I'm happy to take questions.
Thank you, Minister. The first question, Government.
Thank you for attending today.
My question, Minister, is with respect to the amount of money allocated to improving the lives of New Zealand women and your ministry.
So $12.5 million, which includes policy advice on Government policy matters.
Given that, why did you not ask the Ministry to provide you with advice on pay equity changes
or their impact on budget as you state in your answer to post budget question 31?
As I've made very clear in the Parliament on a number of occasions, I didn't seek advice
from the Ministry for Women because the Cabinet and indeed Cabinet committees,
which I belong to, were being given advice by the Lead Agencies being MB and PSA.
And so, sorry, PSC, might confuse those, and they are to be, they are considered by far and away
the Lead Agencies to be advising on pay equity. I was satisfied with that advice that we were
being given. I was very comfortable that a robust and long-standing framework for raising
and solving pay equity claims was going to continue and be enduring, and therefore I took
that advice and was satisfied with it. So as a supplementary, do you as the Minister accept that you
have some of the most expert pay equity advisers in the Ministry for Women? And if so, and they're
part of your policy team, if so, why would the Minister forego getting advice from her own
ministry and instead fall back on the advice of other agencies as she stated? Well, I need
to be very clear. I have 100% confidence and faith in the officials at the Ministry for Women,
but as I have said, on multiple occasions, MB and the PSC are the Lead Agencies on Advising
around Pay Equity. I took that advice and I'm
was satisfied with the advice.
So the lead agencies, but not the experts on pay equity.
They are considered the experts on pay equity.
My next question, as a saa, is given the Ministry for Women
do have the experts on pay equity in their policy team,
have you asked your ministry to monitor the implications
of the pay equity changes, the implications for women,
the implications for those workforces,
have you asked your ministry from here on in
to monitor those changes?
I've got a number of advisory councils
that I draw on for advice
and indeed provide a brilliant resource to me.
Some of those have shown an interest
in providing a reflection,
and so I'm open to discussing with the ministry
as to whether or not we'll take them up on that offer
and we're yet to form a decision on that.
But the minister has not asked her ministry
to monitor the implications
of the pay equity changes moving forward.
Not specifically, no.
Will the minister ask her ministry to do that?
I'm yet to decide.
All right, I do.
Thank you for being here today, Minister.
Do you agree that Māori Pacific, Asian and disabled women
face some of the largest pay equity gaps in Aotearoa
with Pacific women earning 20.9% less than Pākehā men?
And if so, why does the Ministry for Women's Flagship
gender pay gap tool,
that you've said 24,000 visits,
we're so happy to see that.
And within that,
it's funded through the Vote Women's
largest appropriation,
but it doesn't include any intersectional analysis
or ability to report on these pay gaps.
Yeah, really good question.
And you've hit on a really, really important issue.
It is. We have every intention of reiterating.
in reiterating the toolkit. The advice that this toolkit by way of background was designed by business for business.
The advice that we received from those advisors was that at the time of launch measuring ethnic pay gaps
and pay gaps for disabled people was quite difficult. There was no standardised methodology.
So I said to my ministry and I said publicly all the time I was really happy to go out with an imperfect toolkit at the first iteration.
I wanted to get it out there and I wanted to begin the national conversation that we need to have about driving down the pay gap in New Zealand.
And I do say this all the time publicly. I have every intention that the next iteration of this toolkit will measure ethnic pay gap.
And we are hoping that by the end of the year we'll be able to relaunch it with measures and models within that to be able to measure ethnic pay gaps.
That sounds great. Just one supplementary. Thank you.
And so has the minister or the ministry commissioned that advice on how to incorporate that intersectional data into the toolkit?
Yes.
Would you like to go into detail?
Minister said the plan was we get the gender toolkit out and then we move on to other components.
And our next focus was on the ethnic pay gap.
So we have worked underway actively on that currently.
We've also engaged Dr Karanina Sumiu to be part of the team working on that.
So that engagement has happened.
We're going to run a similar process to the one we use for developing the gender pay gap toolkit.
Obviously continuing to work closely with business.
Also businesses who are currently leading out on reporting their ethnic pay gap.
So they'll be part of the work that we're doing and obviously also engaging.
with communities themselves so that we can hear directly from people, and those engagements
will be happening across the country also.
Do you have a question, Eru?
Eru.
Eru.
Thank you.
Sorry, just finding the right button.
Thank you, Mr Chair.
Minister, I'd like to turn my question to the online harm, and in particular your focus
around women and leadership, and I don't know that there would be many women around the table
you're sitting at today that hasn't fallen victim to some of that abuse and bullying that
goes on online.
Can you give us a bit of an update, and we plan to take this?
Thank you for the question.
We've been largely biased by NetSafe, but also have been working in with a number of
public-facing leaders who the Ministry has formed a relationship with.
We launched this particular talk at the local government conference this year, and where there
were a number of women mayors, and I'm sure you yourself would remember your time, who
wanted to talk to it and be involved in it.
As well as a number of broadcasters and other people who hold public-facing roles, it's
had about 7,400 hits, having been launched quite recently, I think it was May, wasn't it, Kelly?
And so, yes, like the pay-git calculator, the intention is that it's an evolving beast.
We will iterate and iterate as and when new technologies, new developments, new advice,
new tools come along.
Thank you, Minister.
It could be itself, but it would be really brief to us.
I know that's right.
Oh, there we go.
Yeah.
Thank you, Minister.
I have a question around the online harm and abuse.
It's quite important to me at the moment I've got a deep, fake bill.
I know that this programme is specifically for women in leadership, but what about young
females who are finding themselves...
creasingly that didn't online harm and as any plans to roll this out to a larger audience
like thinking of our high school girls for example. Well congratulations to the member
on the work that you're leading on that it's really important work to do. Callie do you
want to talk to any specific? Sure. So our focus has been on women in leadership and while
this resource was designed with women in mind we hope it will be useful for anyone who might be
experiencing online harm. We've, like the minister said, we're wanting to continue to add to it.
We've got a video series called Beyond the Keyboard which features seven women. That's had 40,000
views and we're really extending our reach to different people that we haven't connected
with before on social media so I think that's important in terms of getting the message out.
To pick up on your point, yes we are wanting to continue to think about how we can make resources
impactful and supporting young women also. We're specifically doing that in the gender pay gap
space by working with the YWCA to bring a young woman's voice through and I think
thinking about online harm, yes. In the online harm space we're also wanting to
do further work thinking about men as allies and the difference that they can make in supporting
women in their lives regardless of their age to continue to engage with confidence in online spaces.
Thank you Mr Chair. I've got a primary and then probably I'm going to drop
down into the Wahine Māori piece of it but just initially just in terms of
lives of New Zealand women. So I can see in the
Rad integration policy initiative, egg Busen script of派

so the Ministry for Women, we made budget savings of 7.5%, not in the current budget context but a year or so ago, we were able to manage through, it equates to about just over a million dollars.
We have been able to manage through that in different ways, but it hasn't impacted significantly on key deliverables.
We need to operate in an agile way because we have a specific envelope with so many people as part of the team.
So, for example, the Mana-Waheni-Kopapa inquiry-related work is led by Kylie Fletcher.
We continue to prioritise that and also think about how we bring a Waheni Māori lens to all of our work.
I hope that answers the question you had around how has that impacted on our ability to deliver work, particularly with a focus on outcomes for Waheni Māori.
We are at time. So, if there's anything else you want to add though.
OK.
Thank you very much.
